narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school applications only to face this setback feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam the way it did, like sharing the chapters of a story that shaped its view of your journey. At the heart of it, your Year 3 decile of 4 stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward a tougher road ahead, suggesting that even as you progressed through school, your grades placed you mid-pack among peers, which the model weighed heavily as a hurdle for bar success. This built on your Year 1 decile of 1, the next biggest influence, painting a picture of an early struggle to find your academic stride that lingered in the model's lens. Your middle family income added another layer, as it often means fewer resources for the intense prep that top bar passers lean on, nudging the outlook downward. Even with a solid LSAT of 41 and undergraduate GPA of 3.7—impressive markers that most would celebrate—these still tilted toward caution in the model's story, as they didn't quite offset the earlier patterns. Being a full-time white male student rounded out the narrative without shifting the tide much, each detail weaving into a 41% predicted chance of not passing the bar on the first try. It's not that you're not capable or driven; it's that the model spots these combined threads—starting lower in deciles, steady but not surging grades, and resource realities—as echoes of paths where others have stumbled before. The good news? This isn't destiny; many rewrite their stories by targeting those Year 3 grades or seeking extra support. You've got a strong foundation with that UGPA and LSAT—imagine channeling them into targeted habits now. Law school isn't closing its doors forever; it's asking you to fortify those early chapters first. Hang in there, Alex—this model is just one reader, and your next draft could change everything. What small step feels right for you today?
